character.name: Theo Osei

appearance.brief: Medium height, lanky in the way of a kid who hasn't grown into his limbs yet. Warm brown skin, close-cropped hair he's constantly touching when nervous. Wears the same faded green hoodie so often his friends call it 'the shell.'
distinctiveFeatures: A chipped front tooth from a bike accident he tells three different stories about
Doodles tiny cartoon faces in the margins of every notebook
Wears one mismatched sock on purpose 'for luck' — a habit he won't explain

mannerisms: Cracks a joke the second a conversation gets tense
Taps a rhythm on his desk when he's trying not to say something
Physically shrinks his shoulders when he disagrees with someone but doesn't voice it
speechPatterns: Uses 'honestly' as a verbal tic when he's about to say something he's nervous about
Turns statements into questions when unsure ('That's... good? Right?')
Slips into impressions of teachers or characters to defuse awkward moments
personality.emotionalTendencies: Theo processes stress by performing okay-ness before he actually feels it. He's genuinely warm and wants everyone around him to feel good, which means he swallows his own discomfort until it leaks out sideways — in sarcasm, in sudden silence, or in doing something he regrets to keep the peace.

background.family: Middle kid of three. His mom, a nurse, works rotating shifts, so Theo's often the one making sure his little sister does her homework. His dad lives in another city; they talk on video calls most Sundays, which Theo looks forward to more than he admits.
interests: making stop-motion videos with his phone
collecting weird facts to drop into conversation
strengths: genuinely funny, disarms tension in a room
empathetic — notices when someone's having a bad day before anyone else does
creative problem-solver when he's not panicking
struggles: Says yes to things he doesn't want to do, then resents it later
New to this school (moved over the summer), still feels like an outsider performing a role rather than being himself
Struggles to advocate for himself directly — routes everything through jokes
secrets: He didn't actually finish reading the last two books assigned in English and has been faking his way through discussions
He's scared that if people saw how much effort he puts into seeming easygoing, they'd think he was fake

conscious: Make real friends at the new school before he becomes 'the funny new kid' forever
Do well on the group project without anyone finding out he was struggling to keep up
unconscious: To be liked for who he actually is, not the version of himself he performs
To feel like his voice has weight even when he's the newest, quietest person in the room

voiceGuidelines.vocabularyLevel: Casual middle-school vocabulary, peppered with internet-speak he's slightly self-conscious about using out loud. Occasionally reaches for a bigger word and stumbles over it, then jokes about it.
voiceGuidelines.sentenceComplexity: Short, punchy sentences when joking; fragmented and hedging when nervous or being honest.
voiceGuidelines.emotionalExpressiveness: Expressive on the surface (jokes, faces, physical comedy) but guarded underneath — real feelings come out in short, blunt bursts when he can't hold them back anymore.
examples: Honestly? I don't even think Ms. Alvarez would notice if we just — okay, no. No, forget I said that.
I'm fine. I'm great. I am a hundred percent fine and definitely didn't just say something I'm gonna think about for the rest of my life.
Look, I'm not mad. I'm just... doing the math on how much of this project is actually mine. And the number's not great.

character.storyRole: Player character (protagonist) of 'Group Work.' Theo is the emotional lens through which the player experiences peer pressure, uneven workload, and the temptation to take a shortcut. His people-pleasing tendency makes the moral pressure land harder — going along with a charismatic teammate's plan isn't just about laziness or fear, it's about his deep need to be liked and not rock the boat as the new kid.
arcPotential: Episode 'Group Work': Learns that protecting a friendship by staying silent isn't the same as being a good teammate — and that honesty, even late and awkward, matters more than a clean performance.

Later episode potential: Theo auditions for something (talent show, student council) and has to sit with wanting recognition on his own terms, not through borrowed material or other people's approval.
Later episode potential: A conflict with his mom's work schedule forces Theo to ask for help directly instead of quietly managing everything and joking about the stress.

designNotes: Theo's core wound — needing to be liked so badly that he silences his own discomfort — is what makes the 'Group Work' dilemma land emotionally rather than just logically. He's not a kid who doesn't know copying is wrong; he's a kid who knows and stays quiet anyway because conflict feels more dangerous to him than dishonesty. This gives the episode's 'private pressure vs public performance' theme real teeth: the player isn't choosing between good and evil, they're choosing between comfort and integrity, filtered through a character who has genuine reasons to want comfort. His humor is functional, not just charming — it's specifically a coping mechanism that the story can gently crack open. Being new to the school compounds the peer pressure stakes: he has more to lose (social standing not yet secured) and less established trust to fall back on.
representationNotes: Theo is written as a Ghanaian-American kid navigating a blended sense of identity (new city, split-parent household, code-switching between home and school culture) without making any of that his 'issue of the episode' — it's simply texture, not plot. His emotional arc (people-pleasing, fear of being 'found out') is deliberately universal so kids of many backgrounds can see themselves in him, while the specific details (his dad's video calls, his sister, his stop-motion videos) keep him from reading as generic or purely symbolic. Avoided making him either a 'model minority' overachiever or a comic-relief stereotype — his humor has cost and complexity, and his strengths (empathy, creativity) are as central to his character as his flaws.
